content.problemStatement:
You are given a sequence A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​. An ordered pair of indices (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) in this sequence is called a beautiful pair if i≠ji \neq ji=j and Ai−AjAi<Ai−AjAj\frac{A_i - A_j}{A_i} \lt \frac{A_i - A_j}{A_j}Ai​Ai​−Aj​​<Aj​Ai​−Aj​​. (Here, division represents real division, e.g. 52\frac{5}{2}25​ is equal to 2.502.502.50.)

Help Chef find the number of beautiful pairs present in the given sequence.

**Note:** Since the input is large, prefer using fast input methods.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.


Output Format
For each test case, print a single line containing one integer — the number of beautiful pairs present in the given sequence.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the length of the sequence. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the number of beautiful ordered pairs (i, j).

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case frequencies are {4:2, 2:1}. Total ordered pairs = 3*2 = 6. Equal‑value pairs = 2*1 = 2. Beautiful pairs = 6‑2 = 4. The second test case has frequencies {2:2, 8:1, 6:1, 1:1, 5:1}. Total pairs = 6*5 = 30, equal pairs = 2*1 = 2, answer = 28.
All values are distinct, so every ordered pair of different indices is beautiful. N=5 gives 5*4 = 20.
All elements are equal, therefore (Ai‑Aj) = 0 for any pair and the inequality is false. No beautiful pairs.

solution.approach: For a pair (i, j) with Ai = x and Aj = y (x, y > 0) the condition (x‑y)/x < (x‑y)/y simplifies to -(x‑y)^2 < 0, which is true for every x ≠ y and false when x = y. Therefore a beautiful pair is any ordered pair of distinct indices whose values differ. The total number of ordered pairs with i ≠ j is N·(N‑1). From this we subtract the ordered pairs where the two values are equal. If a value v appears f times, it contributes f·(f‑1) non‑beautiful ordered pairs. The answer is: answer = N·(N‑1) – Σ f·(f‑1) over all distinct values. We count frequencies using a hash map (or an array of size 1e6+1 because Ai ≤ 1e6) and compute the sum in O(N) time per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case (reading the array and counting frequencies).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(U) where U is the number of distinct values (≤ 1e6). Using a hash map the extra space is O(number of distinct values).
